head_coach.name: Mike McCarthy
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: West Coast tradition with emphasis on run game, play-action, and ball control; calls his own plays; prioritizes building offense around QB's strengths

offensive_coordinator.name: Brian Angelichio
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: West Coast / McCarthy tree; background as TE coach and passing game coordinator under Kevin O'Connell in Minnesota

defensive_coordinator.name: Patrick Graham
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Bill Belichick / New England Patriots tree; versatile multiple-front coordinator

offense.base_personnel: 11 with diverse personnel mixing (11, 12, 21 all used; McCarthy historically ranked bottom-half in 11-personnel usage because he diversifies groupings more than most)
offense.run_scheme: Inside zone / zone-based with gap concepts mixed in; McCarthy has historically emphasized running the ball to control tempo, though his Green Bay teams rarely had elite rushing attacks
offense.pass_concepts: West Coast quick passing game tied to QB footwork, short-to-intermediate timing routes that stretch defenses horizontally, with vertical shot plays mixed in; Angelichio's Vikings background adds emphasis on downfield passing concepts
offense.rpo_usage: low to moderate — McCarthy is a traditional West Coast play-caller, not an RPO-heavy designer
offense.te_usage: High-value receiving role. Angelichio is a career TE coach who developed T.J. Hockenson (95-catch season), Gary Barnidge (1,043 yards), and Jimmy Graham in GB. Pittsburgh has Pat Freiermuth and Jonnu Smith as dual receiving threats. Expect TEs flexed into slot, featured in 12 personnel passing, and integral to play-action concepts.
offense.rb_usage: McCarthy values the run to 'rest his defense' — expects a primary runner plus a committee element. West Coast system uses RBs as checkdown/flat receivers, moderate receiving role. Jaylen Warren is a capable pass-catcher.

defense.base_front: 3-4 (McCarthy explicitly stated he wants to remain in a 3-4 and build off the existing foundation)
defense.alignment: Nominally odd front (3-4) but Graham is scheme-flexible — in Las Vegas he deployed mostly even (4-down) fronts to maximize Maxx Crosby. With Pittsburgh's T.J. Watt and Nick Herbig as stand-up OLBs, expect a true 3-4 odd front as the base with sub-package 4-2-5 / nickel looks on passing downs.
defense.edge_rush_source: OLB stand-up (T.J. Watt, Nick Herbig as primary edge rushers from a two-point stance in 3-4 alignment)
defense.coverage_base: Primarily zone — Cover 3 foundation with soft zone ('bend but don't break'); Graham is not highly creative in the secondary but disguises fronts well. Limited exotic coverages; relies on front-seven pressure to support coverage.
defense.blitz_tendency: moderate to heavy — Graham incorporates simulated blitzes and creative pressure packages, though his Raiders defenses were not among the heaviest blitzers. Pittsburgh's elite OLB talent (Watt) means generating pressure without needing to blitz as often.
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Patrick Queen, Payton Wilson) are run/coverage players. OLBs (Watt, Herbig) are primary pass rushers. Graham values one LB who can handle the majority of snaps (a la Blake Martinez's 1,063-snap season with the Giants). Graham also historically uses a safety/LB hybrid in the box.
defense.safety_usage: Graham is known for deploying a strong safety/linebacker hybrid — Jabrill Peppers in NYG took more box snaps than the backup ILB. Expect one safety deep and one safety used as a versatile box/slot defender. Jamal Adams filled this hybrid role in Las Vegas in 2025. Minkah Fitzpatrick projects as the deep free safety; the strong safety spot will be a box/hybrid role.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Angelichio's entire NFL career has been built on developing receiving TEs — Hockenson's franchise-record 95-catch season in Minnesota, Barnidge's 1,043-yard Pro Bowl year in Cleveland, and Jimmy Graham's production in GB all came under his coaching. McCarthy's West Coast system uses TEs as featured receiving weapons, not just blockers. Pittsburgh already deploys Freiermuth and Jonnu Smith in pass-heavy roles. Angelichio's scheme will flex TEs into the slot and feature them in the progression reads. An elite receiving TE prospect (e.g., Kenyon Sadiq) would be valued near WR levels in this offense.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: McCarthy explicitly stated the Steelers will remain in a 3-4 defense. In this scheme, OLBs are the primary edge rushers — T.J. Watt is technically an OLB but is a pure pass rusher. Graham has flexibility to use both 3-4 and 4-3 fronts but the Pittsburgh base will feature two stand-up OLB rushers. Any LB prospect with elite pass rush traits (burst, bend, first step) fits perfectly as a stand-up OLB edge rusher in this defense and should be valued at EDGE prices, not traditional LB prices.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: McCarthy's West Coast foundation inherently values backs who can catch out of the backfield — the system uses RBs as checkdown targets, flat receivers, and screen options. However, McCarthy has historically prioritized the ground game and running the ball to control clock over featuring RBs as dynamic receivers. The boost is real but moderate — Pittsburgh wants backs who CAN catch, but won't scheme them as a primary weapon the way a true spread/pass-heavy team would. Jaylen Warren already fills a receiving back role.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: This is a signature element of Graham's defense. With the Giants, Graham deployed Jabrill Peppers as a safety/LB hybrid who took more box snaps than the backup ILB. Peppers recorded career-highs in sacks (2.5), TFLs (8), and passes defended (10) in this role. In Las Vegas, Jamal Adams was converted to a similar LB/safety hybrid. Graham consistently builds his defense around a versatile strong safety who can play in the box, cover TEs/RBs in the flat, blitz, and support the run. A safety prospect with elite tackling, blitz ability, and man coverage traits would be extremely valuable to Pittsburgh's defense.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Pittsburgh's 2026 coaching overhaul creates several clear positional value shifts. On defense, the 3-4 base under Graham means OLB pass rushers are the premium — any LB with elite rush traits should be valued as an EDGE for PIT. Graham's signature hybrid safety/LB role (Peppers in NYG, Adams in LV) means a box safety with tackling/blitz/coverage versatility carries amplified value. On offense, Angelichio is the most TE-centric OC hire in the league — his entire coaching career was spent developing receiving TEs, and McCarthy's West Coast system feeds that. Elite receiving TEs should be valued near WR1 levels for Pittsburgh. The diverse personnel groupings McCarthy favors (not locked into 11 personnel) further enhance TE value since 12-personnel looks will be a significant part of the package. RBs with receiving chops get a moderate bump from the West Coast scheme but this is not a primary pass-game-through-the-backfield offense.
